question: Explain the fundamental steps involved in building a text classifier using supervised learning, from raw text to a trained model.
expectedPoints: Text Preprocessing (e.g., tokenization, stopword removal, stemming/lemmatization)
Feature Extraction (e.g., converting text to numerical features using Bag-of-Words or TF-IDF)
Model Selection (e.g., choosing an algorithm like Naive Bayes, SVM, or Logistic Regression)
Model Training (fitting the chosen algorithm on the vectorized training data)
Evaluation (assessing model performance on a test set using metrics like accuracy, precision, recall)

question: Compare and contrast the Bag-of-Words model with TF-IDF vectorization. What are the key advantages and limitations of each method in the context of text classification?
expectedPoints: Bag-of-Words (BoW): Represents text as a multiset of words, ignoring grammar and word order, focusing only on frequency.
TF-IDF: Enhances BoW by weighting a word's frequency by its inverse document frequency, reducing the importance of common words.
Advantage of BoW: Simplicity and ease of implementation.
Limitation of BoW: It treats all words as equally important, which often isn't the case.
Advantage of TF-IDF: It highlights words that are more discriminative for a specific document.
Limitation of TF-IDF: It can struggle with very similar documents and is still a sparse representation that ignores context.

question: Why is the Naive Bayes algorithm particularly popular for text classification tasks, despite its 'naive' assumption of feature independence?
expectedPoints: Computational Efficiency: It is fast to train and predict, even on very large datasets.
Works well with High-Dimensional Data: It handles the high-dimensional feature space created by text vectorization effectively.

The 'Naive' Assumption: While words are not independent, the model often performs surprisingly well in practice because it captures the frequencies of classes and features well enough for classification.
It serves as a strong and simple baseline model against which more complex models can be compared.
